coredata.dc:title: The CuSCN layer between BiVO4 and NiFeOx for facilitating photogenerated carrier transfer and water oxidation kinetics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jingkun
Sun, Jidong
Liu, Yuliang
Zhang, Xun
Cheng, Kai
Chen, Yupeng
Zhou, Fangzhou
Luo, Jujie
Li, Tianbao
Guo, Junjie
Xu, Bingshe
coredata.dc:description: Modification of oxygen evolution co-catalyst (OEC) on the surface of bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) can effectively improve the kinetics of water oxidation, but it is still limited by the small hole extraction driving force at the BiVO4/OEC interface. Modulating the BiVO4/OEC interface with a hole transfer layer (HTL) is expected to facilitate hole transport from BiVO4 to the OEC surface. Herein, a copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN) HTL is inserted between BiVO4 and NiFeOx OEC to create BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode, resulting in a significant enhancement of photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting performance. From electrochemical analyses and density functional theory (DFT) simulations, the markedly enhanced PEC performance is attributed to the insertion of CuSCN as an HTL, which promotes the extraction of holes from BiVO4 surface and boosts the water oxidation kinetics. The optimal photoanode achieves a photocurrent density of 5.6 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (vs. RHE) and an impressive charge separation efficiency of 96.2 %. This work offers valuable insights into the development of advanced photoanodes for solar energy conversion and emphasizes the importance of selecting an appropriate HTL to mitigate recombination at the BiVO4/OEC interface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hole transfer layer
Copper(I) thiocyanate
BiVO4 photoanode
Photoelectrochemical water splitting
Oxygen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of porous BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx and W-BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes
Synthesis and characterization of BiVO4 photoanodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Basic Research Projects of Shanxi Province
Shanxi-Zheda Institute of Advanced Materials and Chemical Engineering
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shanxi-Zheda Institute of Advanced Materials and Chemical Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Shanxi-Zheda Institute of Advanced Materials and Chemical Engineering (2021SXAT006), the Basic Research Projects of Shanxi Province (20210302123125).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation process for BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode. (b-d) SEM images of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx films. (e) XRD patterns of the BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx films. (f) TEM image of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx. (g) HRTEM image of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx. (h) The SEM-EDS element mappings of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx.

(a) Bi 4f, (b) V 2p, and (c) O 1s XPS spectra of (I) BiVO4, (II) BiVO4/CuSCN and (III) BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx. (d) Cu 2p XPS spectra of BiVO4/CuSCN and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx. (e) Fe 2p and (f) Ni 2p XPS spectra of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx.

(a) LSV curves of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, BiVO4/NiFeOx, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx illuminated with AM 1.5 G light in 0.5 M KBi electrolyte (the experiment was repeated three times, and the error range of the data was retained to two digit after the decimal point). (b) ABPE curves and (c) IPCE curves of the BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx. (d) Photocurrent density vs. time curves at 1.23 V vs. RHE of BiVO4 and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes in 0.5 M KBi electrolyte under AM 1.5 G illumination (100 mW cm−2). Inset in panel (d): the SEM image of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode after 15 h water splitting test. (e) Actual amounts of H2 and O2 evolution of the BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode (the experiment to measure the gas yield was repeated three times). (f) UV − vis spectra, (g) charge separation efficiencies, and (h) charge transfer efficiencies of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx.

(a) EIS curves, (b) Mott − Schottky plots, and (c) PL emission spectra of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx. (d, e) On–off photocurrent decay curves (d) and corresponding electron lifetimes (e) at 1.23 V vs. RHE of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx under chopped illumination (the calculation error of the electron lifetime value was calculated three times, and the error range of the data was maintained to two digits after the decimal point). (f) OCP decay profiles and OCP values. Inset in panel (a): the equivalent circuit model.

Intensity modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) responses of (a) BiVO4, (b) BiVO4/CuSCN, and (c) BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx at various potentials. (d) Rate constants for charge recombination (Krec) and (e) rate constants for charge transfer (Ktrans) of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes. (f) Charge storage capability against applied bias for BiVO4 and BiVO4/CuSCN photoanodes. (The calculation errors for the values of Krec, Ktrans, and charge storage capacity were calculated three times).

(a) LSV curves in the dark and (b) Tafel slope curves of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx. (c) Construction models of BiVO4 (Ⅰ), BiVO4/CuSCN (Ⅱ), and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx (Ⅲ). (d-f) Gibbs free energy changes during OER for (d) BiVO4, (e) BiVO4/CuSCN, and (f) BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx.
(a, b) Schematic illustration of the charge transfer process on BiVO4 and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes.

head.ce:title: The CuSCN layer between BiVO4 and NiFeOx for facilitating photogenerated carrier transfer and water oxidation kinetics

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a CuSCN hole transport layer (HTL) is introduced between BiVO4 and NiFeOx oxygen evolution cocatalyst (OEC) to form BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode. Characterization and DFT calculations suggest that the high PEC performance is attributed to the incorporation of CuSCN as a HTL, which effectively facilitates charge transfer and accelerates the water oxidation kinetics, resulting in an enhanced PEC water splitting performance (5.65 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE).

Modification of oxygen evolution co-catalyst (OEC) on the surface of bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) can effectively improve the kinetics of water oxidation, but it is still limited by the small hole extraction driving force at the BiVO4/OEC interface. Modulating the BiVO4/OEC interface with a hole transfer layer (HTL) is expected to facilitate hole transport from BiVO4 to the OEC surface. Herein, a copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN) HTL is inserted between BiVO4 and NiFeOx OEC to create BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode, resulting in a significant enhancement of photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting performance. From electrochemical analyses and density functional theory (DFT) simulations, the markedly enhanced PEC performance is attributed to the insertion of CuSCN as an HTL, which promotes the extraction of holes from BiVO4 surface and boosts the water oxidation kinetics. The optimal photoanode achieves a photocurrent density of 5.6 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (vs. RHE) and an impressive charge separation efficiency of 96.2 %. This work offers valuable insights into the development of advanced photoanodes for solar energy conversion and emphasizes the importance of selecting an appropriate HTL to mitigate recombination at the BiVO4/OEC interface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is an ideal material for visible-light photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting because of its narrow bandgap (2.4–2.5 eV) and deep valence band edges [1–4]. However, BiVO4 photoanodes struggle with severe charge recombination and slow water-oxidation kinetics due to the weak charge transport properties and limited diffusion lengths for holes (<70 nm), resulting in unsatisfactory PEC performance [5–8]. Various strategies have been proposed to overcome these drawbacks, such as morphology engineering [9], heteroatom doping [10,11], heterostructure construction [12], and loading with an oxygen evolution co-catalyst (OEC) [13]. Among these strategies, loading with OEC can accelerate the water-oxidation kinetics and play an essential role in suppressing surface recombination of BiVO4 photoanodes [14,15]. However, Van de Krol et al. further demonstrated that the modification of BiVO4 surface with highly active OECs does not always achieve exceptional PEC performance, which is attributed to the water-oxidation capacity of the OEC is limited by the inadequate extraction of holes from the photoanode [16].

Working with an engineered interface between OEC and BiVO4 through hole transfer layers (HTLs) is an efficacious approach to facilitate the extraction and transfer of holes [17–20]. For example, inserting black phosphorene as an HTL between BiVO4 and OECs resulted in a remarkable photocurrent density of 4.48 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE [21]. Various materials such as NiOOH [22], FeOOH [23], NiO [24], and Co3O4 [25] have been also applied as HTLs for the storage and transfer of photogenerated holes. At the same time, the HTL also can increase the stability of photoanodes. Taking inspiration from the effectiveness of HTL, the exploration of a suitable HTL material is extremely meaningful for improving the performance of photoanodes.

Copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN) has attracted attention in solar cells due to its wide bandgap (3.4–3.9 eV), low cost, high carrier mobility, good stability, and excellent transmittance [26–30]. Although CuSCN has been effectively utilized as a hole-transport material in solar cells, limited research has been conducted on its potential application as a functional layer for PEC water splitting, with the exception of its use in photocathodes [31] and in n-Si/SiOx/CuSCN photoanodes [32].

In this study, we propose for the first time the insertion of a CuSCN HTL between the BiVO4 photoanode and the OEC layer to construct a BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode for boosting the PEC performance. Experimental characterization and density functional theory (DFT) simulations reveal that the significant augmentation of PEC water splitting performance is credited to the insertion of CuSCN as an HTL, which promotes the hole transfer from BiVO4 to OEC and accelerates the water-oxidation kinetics. The optimized photoanode achieves a photocurrent density of 5.6 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE and a charge separation efficiency of 96.2 %. In addition, the promotion of hole transport from semiconductor to OEC by CuSCN HTL is also available for two other semiconductors (WO3 and TiO2), substantiating the universal function of the CuSCN layer in meliorating the PEC water splitting performance of semiconductor photoanodes.

Potassium iodide (KI, AR, 99 %), bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3, AR, 99 %), p-benzoquinone (AR, 99.5 %), vanadium(IV)oxy acetylacetonate (VO(acac)2, AR, 95 %), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO, AR), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, 96 %), nickel sulfate (NiSO4, AR, 98 %), ferric sulfate (FeSO4, AR, 98 %), sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4, AR, 99.5 %), titanium isopropoxide (AR, 99.0 %), and ammonium oxalate ((NH4)2C2O4, AR, 99.5 %) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN, 98 %) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Nitric acid (HNO3, AR, 68 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, UP, 30 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR, 38 %), ethanol (AR, 99.5 %), and diethyl sulfide (C14H10S, 98 %) were got from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

The fabrication of BiVO4 photoanodes was based on a previous report by Choi et al. [13] Specifically, 0.4 M KI and 0.02 M Bi(NO3)3 were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized (DI) water and HNO3 was added to adjust the pH of the solution to 1.7. Subsequently, 20 mL of a 0.23 M p-benzoquinone ethanol solution was mixed with the above solution. For subsequent electrodeposition, a clean fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO, 1.0 × 2.0 cm2) substrate, an Ag/AgCl (4 M KCl) electrode and a platinum wire electrode were employed as the working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The BiOI films were obtained by electrodeposition in the final solution under a bias voltage of −0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 200 s. After that, 100 μL of a 0.2 M VO(acac)2 DMSO solution was dropped onto the BiOI films and annealed at 450 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. Finally, the BiVO4 photoanodes were immersed in 1 M NaOH solution for 1 h to remove excess V2O5. The bare BiVO4 was rinsed with DI water and air dried at room temperature.

The BiVO4/CuSCN photoanode was fabricated by spin-coating a CuSCN dimethyl sulfide solution (50 µL, 5 mg mL−1) onto the BiVO4 film at 3000 rpm for 45 s, followed by heating at 120 ℃ for 10 min to evaporate the solvent. The thickness of the CuSCN film was controlled by adjusting the rotational speed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of porous BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx and W-BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The NiFeOx was photo-assisted electrodeposited from a 0.4 M FeSO4 and 0.04 M NiSO4 aqueous solution at 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 4 min in a three-electrode cell [33]. The optimal co-catalyst loading was achieved by adjusting the deposition time.

For the preparation of W-BiVO4 and its composite photoanodes, 100 μL of a solution containing 2 mM Na2WO4 and 0.2 M VO(acac)2 was dropped onto the BiOI films, leaving the other growth parameters unchanged. The doping ratio of W in BiVO4 was modified by adjusting the amounts of Na2WO4.

TiO2 nanorod arrays were synthesized according to previous report [34]. First, 0.8 mL of titanium isopropoxide was added to a mixed solution with equal volume (30 mL) of H2O and HCl under continuous stirring. The FTO substrate was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave containing the above solution and treated at 150 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the FTO was washed several times with DI water and ethanol. Finally, the substrate was calcined at 450 °C for 3 h. The CuSCN and NiFeOx layers were prepared on the TiO2 photoanode using the same procedure.

The WO3 nanosheets were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Under ultrasonic assistance, 2 mL of HNO3, 2 mL of 30 % H2O2, and 0.231 g of Na2WO4 were dissolved in 30 mL of DI water to form a precursor solution. Subsequently, 0.2 g (NH4)2C2O4 was introduced into the precursor solution and diluted to 70 mL with DI water. The FTO substrate was immersed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave filled with the above solution and kept at 160 °C for 8 h. Finally, the substrate was annealed at 500 °C for 2 h. The CuSCN and NiFeOx layers were prepared on WO3 photoanode using the same process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of BiVO4 photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure illustrated in Fig. 1 a demonstrates a simple method for the synthesis of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx. Firstly, porous BiVO4 array was synthesized on an FTO substrate. Secondly, CuSCN layer was spin-coated onto the porous BiVO4, resulting into the formation of a BiVO4/CuSCN. Finally, the NiFeOx OEC was electrodeposited on the surface of BiVO4/CuSCN and labeled as BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx.

The morphological changes of the samples at different stages were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). After annealing, the morphology of BiOI underwent a transition from nanosheets (Fig. S1) to a worm-like nanoporous structure (Fig. 1b). A conformal encapsulation layer of CuSCN was formed on the BiVO4 surface through spin-coating process, as illustrated in Fig. 1c. In addition, distinct nanoparticles were observed after the deposition of NiFeOx (Fig. 1d). SEM-energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) characterization of the BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode in Fig. 1h reveals a homogeneous distribution of all elements, affirming successful loading of CuSCN and NiFeOx on the BiVO4 surface. Based on the inserted cross-sectional images in Fig. 1b–d, the thickness of the BiVO4 film is about 1.1 µm.

The crystal structure of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), as presented in Fig. 1e. All diffraction peak corresponded to the monoclinic phase BiVO4 (JCPDS PDF#14-0688) as well as the FTO substrate [35]. Interestingly, no characteristic peaks of CuSCN and NiFeOx were detected in the XRD spectra of BiVO4/CuSCN and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes, which may be too few to be recognized. To analyze the microstructure of the surface-modified CuSCN/NiFeOx, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed. The TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images depicted that the BiVO4 surface was covered with an amorphous CuSCN (∼7 nm) and NiFeOx (∼5 nm) layer, as displayed in Fig. 1f and g. The lattice fringe of 0.47 nm is well matched to the (110) plane of BiVO4 (inset in Fig. 1g).

The chemical bonding properties of the various photoanodes were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Compared with pristine BiVO4, the positive displacement of Bi 4f and V 2p was observed in the XPS spectra of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx and BiVO4/CuSCN (Fig. 2 a and b). Additionally, a slight positive shift of Cu 2p was also detected after the deposition of NiFeOx on BiVO4/CuSCN (Fig. 2d). These results indicate that there is a strong interaction between NiFeOx, CuSCN and BiVO4, which can promote the formation of charge transfer channels [25,36]. In the O 1s spectra, three oxygen chemical states (Fig. 2c) were identified, corresponding to the lattice oxygen (OL), the oxygen deficient region (OV), and the chemically adsorbed or dissociated oxygen (OC), respectively [37,38]. Compared to pristine BiVO4, the relative intensities of the OV peaks for BiVO4/CuSCN and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx gradually increased, contributing to the kinetics of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [25,39]. In the Cu 2p high-resolution XPS spectra, three peaks were assigned to Cu 2p3/2 (931.14 eV), Cu 2p1/2 (950.86 eV), and Cu 2p3/2,sat (939.38 eV), indicating that the Cu in BiVO4/CuSCN exists in the form of Cu+ (Fig. 2d) [40,41]. Moreover, in the Fe 2p XPS spectra, the peaks at 711.15 and 724.56 eV were ascribed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, which can be deconvoluted into Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively (Fig. 2e) [42–44]. Two strong signals dominated by 856.6 and 874.4 eV are identified as Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 of Ni2+, respectively (Fig. 2f) [45–47].

The PEC water oxidation performance was investigated in a typical three-electrode cell under AM 1.5G illumination with 0.5 M KBi solution (pH = 9.3) as electrolyte. By controlling the rotational speed of spin-coating, the loading of CuSCN could be adjusted. The rotational speed for optimal thickness of the CuSCN was determined to be 3000 rpm based on the PEC performance displayed in Fig. S2. An optimum deposition time of 4 min was identified for NiFeOx, as demonstrated in Fig. S3. Based on the optimized preparation conditions mentioned above, the linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) curves of the samples were evaluated, with results presented in Fig. 3 a. Obviously, the BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode exhibits the highest photocurrent density compared to the BiVO4 and BiVO4/CuSCN photoanodes. For BiVO4/CuSCN, the photocurrent density reaches 2.5 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, while the pristine BiVO4 is only 0.95 mA cm−2. Upon loading NiFeOx, the photocurrent densities of BiVO4/NiFeOx and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx increased to 2.9 and 5.1 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, respectively (Fig. 3a and S4). The latter is 5.4-fold higher compared to BiVO4. Furthermore, the PEC performance of the BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode was also measured in seawater (Fig. S5). However, the photocurrent density only reaches 3.6 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, attributed to the competition reactions induced by Cl− [48,49]. Additionally, the introduction of W metal atoms into the BiVO4 lattice resulted in a photocurrent density of 5.6 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE for the W-BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode, as displayed in Fig. S6. Compared with the PEC water splitting performance of BiVO4-based photoanodes modified with HTL materials (Table S1), our prepared photoanodes exhibit higher PEC performance.

The photoconversion efficiency of the photoanode was described by the applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) acquired from the corresponding LSV curves. The results presented in Fig. 3b indicate that the ABPE of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx achieves the highest value of 1.75 % at 0.68 V vs. RHE, surpassing that of BiVO4/CuSCN (0.81 %) and BiVO4 (0.17 %). Additionally, the incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) is an essential indicator for evaluating the PEC performance of the photoanode. Notably, BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx exhibits the highest IPCE value of 79 % at 450 nm (Fig. 3c), which is about 3.3-fold higher than pristine BiVO4. In addition to the PEC water oxidation performance, the long-term durability of the photoanode is also crucial. The photostability of BiVO4 and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx was evaluated at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The photocurrent density of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx maintains good stability within 15 h, while BiVO4 decreases by 47 % within 3 h, indicating the superior durability of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx for PEC water splitting (Fig. 3d). After the stability test, the CuSCN/NiFeOx nanoparticles still covered the surface of BiVO4 well (Fig. S7). The XRD spectra of the BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx films are not significantly different from those before the stability test (Fig. S8). Moreover, gas chromatography was employed to determine the amounts of hydrogen and oxygen generated from BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode during PEC water splitting. The amounts of hydrogen and oxygen are 911 and 448 μmol after a 10 h test, showing a linear increase with the irradiation time (Fig. 3e).

To investigate the potential mechanism behind the enhancement of PEC performance, the photoelectrical characteristics and charge transfer kinetics of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes were examined. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra (Fig. 3f) and Tauc plots (Fig. S9) showed no significant alterations, suggesting that the modifications of CuSCN and NiFeOx layers have minimal impact on light absorption. By measuring the LSV curves in a 0.5 M KBi electrolyte with Na2SO3 as a hole scavenger, the charge transfer (ηtrans) and charge separation (ηsep) efficiencies during the PEC water oxidation process were calculated for the three photoanodes. (Fig. S10). The ηsep curves displayed in Fig. 3g revealed ηsep values of 52.9 %, 61.8 %, and 93.4 % at 1.23 V vs. RHE for BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes, respectively. Surprisingly, the ηsep value is further increased to 96.2 % for the W-BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx sample (Fig. S11). Additionally, the ηtrans values of the three samples are 29.6 %, 67.4 % and 79.2 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3h. The addition of CuSCN and NiFeOx layers significantly enhance the ηsep and ηtrans of BiVO4, inhibiting the surface-accumulated hole to oxidize the Bi sites on the surface of BiVO4, which is consistent with stability test results [20,50]. While CuSCN had a modest impact on improving ηsep compared to NiFeOx, its contribution to boosting ηtrans was more pronounced. Through the above analysis, the effect of CuSCN on inhibiting carrier recombination is not significant, and it may mainly act as a role in promoting the transfer of the hole as well as catalysis.

The Mott–Schottky (M−S) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analyses were conducted to gain a deeper understanding of the charge transfer behavior of BiVO4-based photoanodes. Among the three photoanodes, the BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode possesses the lowest charge transfer resistance, with the CuSCN layer playing a more significant role in reducing the transfer resistance compared to NiFeOx (Fig. 4 a). As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the M−S plots of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes exhibit positive slopes, indicating their n-type characteristics. Notably, the slope of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode is smaller than those of the BiVO4 and BiVO4/CuSCN photoanodes, suggesting enhanced electronic conductivity upon the incorporation of NiFeOx and CuSCN. Moreover, the negative shift in flat band potential (EFB) was observed in the M−S plots after the modification of CuSCN and NiFeOx, which was consistent with the lower onset potentials observed in the LSV (Fig. 3a). The M−S plot for the thickened CuSCN film on FTO substrate displayed a negative slope and an EFB value of 2.19 V, which represents the p-type characteristic (Fig. S12). Combining the results of UV–vis absorption spectra of BiVO4 and CuSCN (Fig. S13), the built-in electric field (BEF) formed by the p-n junction on BiVO4/CuSCN can be considered as the reason for the enhancement of photogenerated charge separation and transfer.

To further verify the consequences of carrier separation, the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was examined. As shown in Fig. 4c–e, the PL intensity gradually decreases and the transient time constant (τ) increases sequentially with the successive addition of CuSCN and NiFeOx layers, indicating the effective suppression of carrier recombination [51]. Furthermore, to validate the presence of a robust BEF, the open-circuit photovoltage (OCP) and OCP transient decay were measured [52,53]. The OCP values of the photoanodes follow the order BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx (0.44 V) > BiVO4/CuSCN (0.35 V) > BiVO4 (0.23 V) (Fig. 4h and g). This is attributed to the effective passivation of the surface states on BiVO4 by the CuSCN and NiFeOx layers, which is also confirmed by photoelectrochemical impedance spectroscopy (PEIS) testing (Figs. S14 and 15) [54–56].

To elucidate the mechanism behind the enhancement of PEC performance by CuSCN, intensity modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) was employed to investigate the charge transfer process and carrier recombination kinetics. Fig. 5 a–c present the IMPS responses of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx at different potentials. The semicircle in the first quadrant of the IMPS plot reflects the competition between minority carrier transfer and electron-hole recombination at the interface, while the semicircle in the fourth quadrant signifies the combination of charge transfer and relaxation within the electrode [16,57]. The ratio of rate constants for charge recombination (Krec)/ rate constants for charge transfer (Ktrans) is small, signifying that charge transfer is faster than charge recombination, which correlates positively with the radius of semicircle in the first quadrant [58]. In comparison, the BiVO4 photoanode shows a large semicircle radius in the first quadrant, signifying that serious charge recombination exists on the surface of BiVO4. After the CuSCN modification, the semicircle radius of BiVO4/CuSCN in the first quadrant and Krec values (Fig. 5d) further decrease with increasing applied voltage, but not significantly. However, the Ktrans values of BiVO4/CuSCN are higher than that of pristine BiVO4 throughout the applied voltage, indicating the enhanced charge transfer capability. From the above analysis, it can be seen that the CuSCN layer primarily plays a role in facilitating hole transfer from the surface of BiVO4. Additionally, the BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode exhibits the smallest semicircle radius (Fig. 5c), the lowest Krec (Fig. 5d), and the highest Ktrans (Fig. 5e) among the three samples due to the hole transfer capability of the CuSCN layer.

To explore the hole transfer capability of CuSCN, the transient cathode current densities of BiVO4 and BiVO4/CuSCN photoanodes were recorded under light-off conditions. As demonstrated in Fig. 5f and S16, the BiVO4/CuSCN photoanode expresses a higher transient cathode current density than BiVO4, indicating a greater number of holes are transferred from BiVO4 to CuSCN [21,25]. Additionally, the CuSCN and NiFeOx OEC layers were also prepared on the surfaces of TiO2 and WO3 photoanodes (Figs. S17–20). The enhanced photocurrent density observed for the TiO2/CuSCN/NiFeOx and WO3/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes (Figs. S21–22), confirms the universal applicability of CuSCN in improving the performance of various photoanode.

To further validate the OER activity of CuSCN and NiFeOx, the LSV curves of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanodes were measured in the dark. The onset potential of the electrode decreased and the current growth became steeper with the sequential addition of CuSCN and NiFeOx layers to the electrode surface, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The Tafel slope of the BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode (114 mV dec−1) is lower than that of BiVO4/CuSCN (173 mV dec−1) and BiVO4 (260 mV dec−1) photoanodes (Fig. 6b). These results indicate that the modified CuSCN and NiFeOx OEC both contribute to the improvement of water oxidation kinetics of BiVO4. In order to gain a deeper understanding of the role of CuSCN in enhancing the OER activity, DFT simulations of BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx were performed during the OER process (Fig. 7 c and S23–25). The OER process involves four crucial reaction stages with various intermediates (*OH, *O and *OOH, where * denotes the active site of catalyst) [59–61]. The rate-determining step (RDS) for these three photoanodes is the second step (*OH → *O), as indicated by the free energy changes during the OER process (Fig. 7d–f). The energy barrier (η) of the RDS for BiVO4, BiVO4/CuSCN, and BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx are 1.02 eV, 0.77 eV and 0.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 7d–f). This indicates that the introduction of CuSCN and NiFeOx both further facilitate the kinetics of the PEC water splitting reaction, which also verifies the results of dark current experiment.

According to the above analysis, the potential mechanism for enhancing PEC performance of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode is illustrated in Fig. 7b. The lower PEC performance of the pristine BiVO4 photoanode is ascribed to the severe carrier recombination and the sluggish reaction kinetics (Fig. 7a). By contrast, the significantly ameliorative PEC performance of BiVO4/CuSCN/NiFeOx photoanode attributes to the synergistic effect of the hole transfer ability of CuSCN layer as well as the high catalytic activity of NiFeOx layer.

In summary, we have demonstrated that the copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN) can serve as an effective hole transfer layer (HTL) in BiVO4/NiFeOx photoanode to enhance the performance of photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. The experimental characterizations and density functional theory simulations indicate that the incorporation of p-type CuSCN HTL can significantly facilitate the hole transport from BiVO4 to NiFeOx and accelerate the kinetics of water oxidation. The optimized photoanode achieves a photocurrent density of 5.6 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (vs. RHE) and a high charge separation efficiency of 96.2 %, which are superior to the majority of BiVO4-based photoanodes reported in recent years. Furthermore, the generic application of the CuSCN layer in enhancing the performance of other semiconductor photoanodes is also verified. Our work demonstrates the promising applicability of CuSCN HTL in solar-driven energy conversion and provides a reliable method to design efficient PEC water splitting photoanodes.

Jingkun Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jidong Sun: Writing – original draft, Validation. Yuliang Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation. Xun Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation. Kai Cheng: Writing – original draft. Yupeng Chen: Writing – original draft. Fangzhou Zhou: Writing – original draft. Jujie Luo: Writing – original draft. Tianbao Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Junjie Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Bingshe Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.
